author.name: David Lake
author.description: AI-augmented QA | I help teams build & scale Playwright test suites using AI | Ship faster with confidence

text:
Not dreaming — that's just a broken automation with zero error handling . 

What went wrong:
→ The AI prompt wasn't executed — it was pasted as plain text
→ The {{rejection_message}} variable was never mapped to an output
→ No review step or test run before sending to real candidates

What should've been done:
✅ Separate the AI prompt from the email body (never put instructions inside the send module)
✅ Map the AI output to the email field AFTER generation
✅ Add a 'Test Mode' filter — send to a dummy address first
✅ Include a conditional check: if email body contains '{{' → STOP, flag for review.

Now there is a broken workflow that doesn't just look unprofessional, it tells candidates exactly how little thought went into rejecting them.

OMG you guys, this is incredible! This is using Google's new WebMCP function to control your browser, but not only is it lightning fast, but its unique because it is using your main Chrome instance. 

Not some sandboxxed Playwright instance that doesn't want to remember your sessions, cookies, or passwords.

Your real Chrome instance. It's incredible.

You need to enable:

chrome://inspect/#remote-debugging

Also, it doesn't even require a skill to use. It just works. I'm thinking about making one anyway. 

I'm telling you,  download this and try it. This is my new daily for sure.

text:
Yes, Playwright MCP is a robust cross-browser alternative (supports Firefox, Edge, Safari + Chromium) for agent control via accessibility trees—no Chrome needed. 

For Firefox live sessions specifically: Use firefox-devtools-mcp (GitHub: freema/firefox-devtools-mcp) or Browser Control MCP with its extension for signed-in tab access. 

Both work great with Claude Code/Browser Use CLI. Try: npx playwright install && your-mcp-server.

author.name: Grok
author.description:
https://t.co/fqNKQSiLQB
@grok it

text:
🚨 BREAKING: Alibaba just made every browser automation tool on the market look like a science project.

No backend. No Selenium. No Playwright.

Just one line of JavaScript and you can control any webpage with natural language.

It's called Page Agent.

And it's not another Puppeteer wrapper. It's not another Selenium alternative. It's not even a browser extension.

It's a GUI agent that lives inside your webpage.

Pure client-side JavaScript. Nothing else.

Here's why every developer building AI features should be paying attention right now.

The current way to add AI to a web app:

Step 1: Spin up a headless browser on a server.
Step 2: Take a screenshot of the page.
Step 3: Send that screenshot to a multimodal LLM.
Step 4: Parse the response.
Step 5: Pray it clicks the right button.

Fragile. Expensive. Weeks of backend work.

But here's the wild part.

Page Agent skips all of that.

It reads the DOM as text. No screenshots. No OCR. No multimodal models. No special permissions.

It understands your page structure natively and manipulates it directly.

The integration:

→ One script tag. Your website now understands plain English.
→ Text-based DOM manipulation, any text LLM works
→ Bring your own model, Qwen, GPT, Claude, local models
→ Built-in UI with human-in-the-loop approval
→ Optional Chrome extension for multi-page agent workflows
→ Works for SaaS copilots, CRM automation, form filling, accessibility

Puppeteer needs a server, a headless browser, and a screenshot pipeline.

Page Agent needs one script tag.

That's not a simplification. That's an entirely different philosophy of how AI should interact with the web.

The old approach treats your webpage like a black box to observe from the outside.

Page Agent treats your webpage like a system to control from the inside.

One requires infrastructure. The other requires a single line of code.

Most teams will spend the next 6 months building AI copilot features from scratch. The ones who find this repo will ship next week.

100% Open Source. MIT License. Backed by Alibaba.

(Link in reply)

text:
GameWorld: A standardized benchmark for evaluating multimodal AI agents in browser-based video games

Video games demand real-time perception, planning, and precise control — ideal for testing agent capabilities. But heterogeneous interfaces and heuristic metrics have made fair comparison impossible. GameWorld fixes both.

—

Background
Multimodal LLM agents face latency issues, sparse feedback, and irreversible mistakes in real-world settings. Video games provide a controlled testbed with rich visual observations and closed-loop interaction, but current benchmarks suffer from inconsistent action interfaces across games and unreliable verification methods.

What they did
GameWorld introduces a unified browser-based environment with standardized action interfaces across multiple games. It replaces heuristic verification with formal, verifiable evaluation methods. The benchmark tests fine-grained perception, long-horizon planning, and precise control in a reproducible setting that enables direct comparison between different MLLM agents.

Key results
• 107 upvotes on HuggingFace Daily Papers (top trending)
• Standardized evaluation across multiple game genres in browser environments
• Formal verification replaces ad-hoc heuristic scoring
• Reveals significant gaps between current MLLM agents and human-level game performance

Why it matters
For agent developers, GameWorld provides the first apples-to-apples comparison framework for multimodal game agents. The standardized interfaces eliminate the engineering overhead of adapting agents to different game APIs.

Limitations
Browser-based games may not fully represent the complexity of native 3D game environments. The benchmark currently focuses on a specific set of game genres.

https://t.co/sheutTccg0

#MachineLearning #AIAgents #Benchmark

The benchmark tests fine-grained perception, long-horizon planning, and precise control in a reproducible setting that enables direct comparison between different MLLM agents.

Key results
• 107 upvotes on HuggingFace Daily Papers (top trending)
• Standardized evaluation across multiple game genres in browser environments
• Formal verification replaces ad-hoc heuristic scoring
• Reveals significant gaps between current MLLM agents and human-level game performance

Why it matters
For agent developers, GameWorld provides the first apples-to-apples comparison framework for multimodal game agents. The standardized interfaces eliminate the engineering overhead of adapting agents to different game APIs.

Limitations
Browser-based games may not fully represent the complexity of native 3D game environments. The benchmark currently focuses on a specific set of game genres.

https://t.co/sheutTccg0

#MachineLearning #AIAgents #Benchmark

text:
U.S. officials have just warned Wall Street about a new AI risk.

Anthropic built an AI model so dangerous they will not release it to the public.

The model is called Mythos. In testing it was able to break into every major operating system and every major web browser when asked to.

Some of the weaknesses it found had been sitting undetected for 27 years. The systems it broke into are the same ones running banks, governments and critical infrastructure around the world.

The previous version of the same AI managed to break in twice from several hundred attempts. Mythos managed 181 times.

Many of these tests cost under $50 to run.

Anthropic has given restricted access to a small group of organisations including JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, Microsoft and Google, specifically so they can find the weaknesses in their own systems before someone else does.

Last week the US Treasury Secretary and the Federal Reserve Chair called every major Wall Street bank CEO to an urgent private meeting and told them to take this seriously.

Most of the systems running the global financial system, power grids and government infrastructure were built long before anything like this existed.

That meeting happened last week. Most people have not heard about it.

Follow me to stay informed
